coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence probe of sulfur quantum dots for sensitive detection of copper ions in Paris polyphylla var. yunnanensis
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Anqi
Yang, Xiaofang
Xia, Ting
He, Daichun
Zhang, Rui
Li, Zongtao
Yang, Shengchun
Liu, Yong
Wen, Xiaodong
coredata.dc:description: The excessive residue of copper ion (Cu2+) has an important impact on the quality of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM). In this work, a sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) fluorescent probe was developed for rapid and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in Paris polyphylla. Smith var. yunnanensis (Franch.) Hand. -Mazz. (P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis). SQDs were synthesized by the “assemble-fission” method. The synthesized SQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and their fluorescence properties were studied. The prepared SQDs have advantages of ultra-small size, good optical properties and excellent stability. The addition of Cu2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of SQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between Cu2+ and SQDs. The detection linear range of this method was 20–200 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was 6.78 μM. It has high selectivity in the simultaneous presence of other ions, and the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was discussed in detail. Finally, the prepared SQDs were applied to the detection of actual samples of P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis with satisfactory results, which confirmed the great potential of SQDs in the actual measurement of Cu2+ in TCM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
Copper ion
Fluorescence probes
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism of Cu2+ quenching SQDs
Determination of Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Fund Project of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Yunnan Provincial Department of Education Science Research Fund Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Xiaodong Wen and Yong Liu gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this project from the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 21964001] and [No. 22176021], and the Doctoral Launching Program of Dali University [No. KY2096107940], and the Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects [No. 202101AU070140] and Scientific Research Fund Project of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education [Grant 2022Y818]. The authors especially thank Professor Haifeng Li of Dali University for his help in the identification of traditional Chinese medicine in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of SQDs and schematic diagram of Cu2+ detection by SQDs.
(a) TEM image of SQDs. (b) HRTEM image of SQDs. (c) Histogram of the size distribution of SQDs. (d) XPS spectra of SQDs. (e) High-resolution spectrum of S 2p. (f) FTIR spectra of SQDs and PEG-400.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs), fluorescence emission (Em), and excitation spectra (Ex) of SQDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of SQDs at different excitation wavelengths (350–430 nm). (c) Effect of different pH conditions on fluorescence intensity of SQDs. (d) Effect of different ion intensity conditions on fluorescence intensity of SQDs.

(a) Relative fluorescence intensity of SQDs in the presence of different ions. Conditions: 0.75 mL SQDs, 2.0 mL of 300 μM Cu2+ solution; other ions, 300 μM. (inset: the relative fluorescence intensity of SQDs in the presence of low concentrations of Cu2+, Ni2+ and Cd2+. Conditions: 0.75 mL SQDs, 2.0 mL of 40–80 μM Cu2+, Ni2+, Cd2+ solution). (b) Optimization for detection pH. Conditions: 0.75 mL SQDs, 2.0 mL 200 μM Cu2+ solution (preparation of PBS with different pH values). (c) Optimization for detection temperature. Conditions: 0.75 mL SQDs, 2.0 mL 200 μM Cu2+ solution. (d) Optimization for detection time. Conditions: 0.75 mL SQDs, 2.0 mL 200 μM Cu2+ solution. (e) Emission spectra of SQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 300, 350, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μM). (f) Calibration curve of Cu2+. Conditions: 0.75 mL SQDs, 2.0 mL of different concentrations of Cu2+ solution (20–200 μM).
(a) Fluorescence decay curves of SQDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs) of Cu2+, fluorescence emission (Em), and excitation spectra (Ex) of SQDs.
Comparison of Cu2+ detected by different fluorescent probes.
Method reproducibility (intra-day precision and inter-day precision).
Determination of Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A fluorescence probe of sulfur quantum dots for sensitive detection of copper ions in Paris polyphylla var. yunnanensis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Dali University, Dali, Yunnan 671000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Dali University, Dali, Yunnan 671000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel turn-off fluorescent probe of SQDs based on inner filter effect was developed and used as an alternative analytical method for the detection of Cu2+ residues among Chinese medicinal materials.

The excessive residue of copper ion (Cu2+) has an important impact on the quality of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM). In this work, a sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) fluorescent probe was developed for rapid and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in Paris polyphylla. Smith var. yunnanensis (Franch.) Hand. -Mazz. (P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis). SQDs were synthesized by the “assemble-fission” method. The synthesized SQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and their fluorescence properties were studied. The prepared SQDs have advantages of ultra-small size, good optical properties and excellent stability. The addition of Cu2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of SQDs due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between Cu2+ and SQDs. The detection linear range of this method was 20–200 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was 6.78 μM. It has high selectivity in the simultaneous presence of other ions, and the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was discussed in detail. Finally, the prepared SQDs were applied to the detection of actual samples of P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis with satisfactory results, which confirmed the great potential of SQDs in the actual measurement of Cu2+ in TCM.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+) can assist in the synthesis of hemoglobin and collagen, regulate cell metabolism, and plays an irreplaceable role in the human body [1,2]. However, excessive intake of Cu2+ will have adverse effects on human health, such as heart disease [3], gastrointestinal disorders [4], Alzheimer’s disease [5], skin diseases and Parkinson’s disease [6]. Therefore, the quantitative determination of Cu2+ is of great significance. Paris polyphylla. Smith var. yunnanensis (Franch.) Hand. -Mazz. (P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis) is a perennial herb [7]. Its dried rhizomes are recorded as “Chonglou” in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia [8]. It is most concentrated in Yunnan and is one of the main medicinal components of Yunnan Baiyao, Gongxuening Capsules and other well-known traditional Chinese medicine preparations [9,10]. P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis plays an important role in pharmacology, such as antitumor [11], hemostasis [12], antifungal activity [13], antiaging [14], disease resistant protozoa [15], and so on. However, it is reported that the content of heavy metals in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) exceeds the standard due to environmental pollution, and the content of Cu2+ in medicinal materials of P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis in some areas seriously exceeds the limit standard of Chinese Pharmacopoeia [16], that is, Cu2+ limit shall not exceed 20 mg/kg [17]. It seriously affects the quality and is not conducive to its healthy development as a medicinal plant. Therefore, it is imperative to monitor the content of Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis.

The commonly used methods for Cu2+ in TCM detection have been reported as inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [18], flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [19], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [20]. However, the sensitivity of FAAS is relatively low, and the sample pretreatment is complex. ICP-OES and ICP-MS have problems of high sampling requirement and detection cost. In response to the above, researchers have investigated several fluorescent probes for the rapid, sensitive detection of Cu2+. Fluorescent probes that can be used to detect Cu2+ have been developed including semiconductor quantum dot probes, inorganic nanomaterial probes, biomolecular probes, and organic molecular probes, such as CdTe quantum dot probes [21], gold nanocluster probes [22], peptide-based fluorescent probes [23], and naphthalimide based fluorescent probes [24]. However, these methods still suffer from the problems of containing toxic heavy metal elements, high synthesis cost, complex synthesis, and poor stability, which greatly limits their application in practical samples.

Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fluorescent probe with high water solubility, good fluorescence stability and low toxicity to detect Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis. Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs), as a new fluorescent material, have the characteristics of rich raw materials [25], low toxicity [26], good water solubility [27], good biocompatibility and natural antibacterial [28]. In recent years, it has gained great development in related research fields (such as ion detection). For example, Tan’s synthesized SQDs with good dispersion and stability by H2O2 assisted, and after the introduction of Cr6+ into SQDs, the quantum dots fluorescence were quenched due to the presence of inner filter effect (IFE), thus realizing the detection of Cr6+ in environmental water samples [29]. Qiao’s research team prepared SQDs using polyethylene glycol 1000 (PEG-1000) as a passivation agent, and realized the fluorescence method and colorimetric method to detect Hg2+ based on the fluorescence quenching effect of Hg2+ on SQDs [30]. Li and his colleagues found that based on the photoelectron transfer effect, the introduction of Co2+ would lead to the fluorescence quenching of L-cysteine modified SQDs. They prepared SQDs into paper sensors and established dual channels of fluorescence and colorimetry [31]. Huang’s group used SQDs “on–off-on” probes to detect Fe3+, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 102 nM [32]. Lu’s research group realized the colorimetric fluorescence detection of Fe2+ in food based on the fluorescence quenching caused by the complexation between Fe2+ and SQDs [33]. In summary, SQDs have the characteristics of rapid, high sensitivity, strong anti-interference ability and high stability in the detection of metal ions, and have great application prospects in the detection of practical samples. So far, the analytical application of SQDs is still in its infancy, and the application of SQDs to the analysis of Cu2+ in complex TCM have not been reported.

In this work, SQDs were synthesized by the “assemble-fission” method using sublimated sulfur as the sulfur source and PEG-400 as the passivation agent, and were characterized and tested for fluorescence probes application. The synthesized SQDs had ultra-small size, good optical properties and excellent stability. Based on the IFE, the synthesized SQDs fluorescent probe could detect Cu2+. In addition, the method was applied to the detection of Cu2+ in TCM samples, which proved the feasibility of the fluorescent probes in practical application.

The raw materials polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400, Aladdin), cupric chloride anhydrous (CuCl2, Aladdin, 99%), chromium (III) nitrate nonahydrate (CrN3O9·9H2O, Aladdin, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, 99%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, 99%), sodium chloride (NaCl, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, 99%), sublimated sulfur (S, Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent, 99%), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent, 99%), zinc sulfate (ZnSO4·7H2O, Chengdu Chemical Reagent, 99%), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2, Shanghai Reagent Factory, 99%) were used without any further purification.

Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and luminescence spectra of the synthesized SQDs were recorded through UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) and F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan) spectrometers. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscopy. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using an Axis Supra X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectra of cyan SQDs and PEG-400 were recorded by Nicolet 380 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo, USA). Time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) decay curves (excitation wavelength 410 nm) were measured through the exploitation of FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The samples were digested by microwave digestion system (Anton Paar, China).

The synthesis of SQDs was performed according to previously reported study [25]. The specific synthesis process was as follows. First of all, PEG-400 (3.0 mL), NaOH (4.0 g), sublimated sulfur powder (1.4 g) were sequentially added to a round-bottomed flask which containing 50 mL of ultra pure water. The mixed solution was then stirred and refluxed at 70 °C for 72 h. Then the sublimated sulfur powder gradually dissolved during the heating process, and the solution transformed from colorless to transparent yellow. Finally, the mixed solution after the reaction was centrifuged at 12,000 round per minute for 10 min in order to remove huge insoluble particles, and the liquid supernatant was collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Firstly, 0.75 mL of SQDs solution and a known with different concentration of Cu2+ standard solution (preparation of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) buffer solution with pH 7) were added to a 5.0 mL centrifugal tube. The mixed solution was then mixed thoroughly for 5 min at room temperature and then tested with a fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm (Fig. 1 ).

The samples were purchased from Dali medicinal materials market in Yunnan Province and identified by Prof. Haifeng Li of Dali University as P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis. The sample was firstly weighed precisely 0.5000 g, and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene digestion tank with 8.0 mL HNO3, 2.0 mL H2O2 and 2.0 mL ultra pure water. The digestion tank was then installed and placed in a microwave digestion device. The digestion procedure was summarized in Table 1 . After digestion, the digestion solution was adjusted to 100.0 mL with ultra pure water to obtain the test solution. The sample detection procedures were similar to the above fluorescence determination method of Cu2+.
Three characterization methods were used to verify the successful synthesis of the prepared SQDs.

By observed the TEM images of SQDs, the results were shown in Fig. 2 a, and the prepared SQDs had a uniform nearly spherical morphology. Clear lattice stripes of SQDs could be observed by HRTEM images (Fig. 2b) with a lattice spacing of 0.31 nm, which was in accordance with the standard card JCPDS NO. 83–2285 (313) crystal plane. In addition, the average particle size of SQDs was about 7.5 ± 0.2 nm and the particle size was distributed between 4.2 nm and 10.7 nm, as shown in Fig. 2c.

The element composition of the prepared SQDs could be analyzed by XPS. The XPS pattern (Fig. 2d) indicated that SQDs contain three main elements (S, C and O). The existence of C and O strong peaks could be attributed to PEG-400 on the surface of SQDs in the form of passivation agent and stabilizing agent. The corresponding XPS high-resolution S 2p spectra (Fig. 2e) shown that the binding energies of 164.5 and 165.4 eV were assigned to atomic sulfur. From these data, it can be seen that the surface of the prepared SQDs were composed of atomic sulfur [26,34].

The structural features of SQDs were further investigated by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2f). The peak at 3471 cm–1 was corresponding to –OH and the band centered at 1650 cm–1 was attributed to C=O. There was an asymmetric stretching vibration absorption peak of –CH at 2884 cm–1. It could be seen that the bending vibration of SQDs at 1450 cm–1 was C-H, 1110 cm–1 and 925 cm–1 were C-O-H or C-O-C stretching vibration, respectively. The characteristic peaks at these places were very consistent with the characteristic peak positions of PEG-400 [35,36]. The characteristic absorption peaks of S atomic in SQDs could be observed at 663 and 570 cm–1, which belonged to the stretching vibration of S-O and S-S, respectively [37]. Compared to the FTIR spectra of SQDs and PEG-400, it could be found that there were no new absorption peaks in the infrared spectra of SQDs, and the infrared absorption peaks of SQDs and PEG-400 were completely consistent. Therefore, it could be seen that PEG was uniformly attached to the surface of SQDs through physical action in the synthesis interaction, and there was no chemical interaction between them.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, it could be observed that SQDs had two UV absorption peaks, one of the two absorption peaks was at 215 nm, which was caused by the n→σ* transition of unbound heteroatomic sulfur, and another absorption peak was near 264 nm, which was caused by the n→π* transition of atomic sulfur [37]. Furthermore, from the fluorescence emission spectra of SQDs (Fig. 3b), it could be observed that the fluorescence intensity increased when the excitation wavelength increased from 350 nm to 410 nm. When the excitation wavelength was greater than 410 nm, the corresponding emission wavelength was red shifted from 499 nm to 503 nm, and there was an excitation dependent PL feature. This typical excitation dependent fluorescence emission phenomenon was consistent with previous reports [27]. The emission peak was at 499 nm under the best excitation wavelength of 410 nm excitation.

In practice, the influence of complicated environmental variables on the luminescence of SQDs must be considered, so the photostability of SQDs under different conditions were further investigated. It could be observed from Fig. 3c that the luminescence of SQDs would not be affected by changing the pH conditions (pH = 5, 5.5, 6, 6.5, 7, 7.5). This was due to the change of pH values (pH = 5–7.5) did not cause the change of surface charge of SQDs, and the particle size of SQDs was small and mutually exclusive, which determined that it had good optical stability. Therefore, the luminescence of SQDs was hardly affected by environmental pH. Meanwhile, the effect of different ionic strengths on the fluorescence intensity was investigated by adding NaCl to the SQDs solution. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity was basically unchanged in the NaCl solution (0–1 M), indicating that the ionic strength had little effect on the luminescence of SQDs. These results indicated that SQDs had good stability in acidic and alkaline environments and under different ionic strength conditions, and were of good application in the detection of practical samples.

In order to verify the selectivity of SQDs for Cu2+, the effects of other ions (Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cr3+, PO4 3–, SO4 2–, and CO3 2–) on the detection were studied. The concentration of Cu2+ was 300 μM, and other cations and anions were 300 μM. As shown in Fig. 4 a (F indicated the fluorescence intensity after adding other ions, F0 indicated the fluorescence intensity without adding other ions), after adding a series of other ions, Cd2+ and Ni2+ would slightly reduce the relative fluorescence intensity of SQDs. But the quenching effect was not obvious compared with Cu2+, and at low concentrations (40–80 μM) of ionic solutions (inset of Fig. 4a), it could be seen that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of SQDs by Cu2+ still exists, but Cd2+ and Ni2+ hardly affect the luminescence of SQDs in this concentration range. Therefore, the effect of Cd2+ and Ni2+ on the detection was almost negligible in practical applications. In addition, the ions Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cr3+, PO4 3–, SO4 2– and CO3 2– had little or no effect on the relative fluorescence intensity of the SQDs. These results indicated that the SQDs had high selectivity for the detection of Cu2+. It further demonstrated the potential value of this fluorescent probe in the analysis of real samples.

For the subsequent work, the pH value, mixing time and temperature were optimized. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the Cu2+ solution was prepared with PBS buffer solution with different pH values and added to SQDs. The fluorescence intensity of the system reached the lowest value at pH = 7. The subsequent experiments used PBS solution with pH = 7 to prepare Cu2+ solutions. The temperature of the detection system was optimized. As depicted in Fig. 4c, different temperatures (5–60 °C) had little effect on the fluorescence quenching of SQDs, and then Cu2+ was detected at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity of SQDs at different mixing times after adding Cu2+ solution was shown in Fig. 4d, from which it could be seen that the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly within 0–5 min, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution could be stabilized after 5 min, then chose 5 min as the best mixing time.

Subsequently, different concentrations of Cu2+ solution (0–1000 μM) were added to the SQDs, as shown in Fig. 4e. The addition of Cu2+ solution affected the fluorescence intensity of SQDs, and the higher the concentration of Cu2+ solution, the more obvious the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Fig. 4f shown the linear relationship between Cu2+ concentration (20–200 μM) and relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0 , F indicated the fluorescence intensity after adding Cu2+, F0 indicated the fluorescence intensity without adding Cu2+). The linear regression equation was F/F0 = 0.9159–0.00162x, the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9952, it could be concluded that the LOD was 6.78 μM, and the detection linear range was 20–200 μM. Compared with the maximum residue limit of Cu2+ in TCM (20 mg/kg) prescribed in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia [17], the LOD of this method was lower than this value. Therefore, the sensitivity of this method was sufficient for the detection of Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis. Compared with other fluorescent probes (Table 2 ), the present method had the advantages of simple operation, abundant raw materials and high sensitivity.

Next, the reproducibility of the method was investigated by intra-day precision and inter-day precision. Intra-day precision was performed five times on the same day used the same experimental conditions and methods at Cu2+ concentrations of 40, 60, and 80 μM. Inter-day precision was measured by performing measurements under the same conditions for five consecutive days and then calculating the relative standard deviation (RSD). The experimental results were shown in Table 3 , and all RSD values were within the acceptable range (RSD < 2.2%), demonstrating the reproducibility and accuracy of the method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of Cu2+ quenching SQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the mechanism of fluorescence quenching action, the fluorescence lifetime of SQDs with and without the addition of Cu2+ were firstly investigated. The fluorescence attenuation curve of SQDs and SQDs-Cu2+ are shown in Fig. 5 a, which gives an average fluorescence lifetime of 3.66 and 3.48 ns, respectively. It could be found that Cu2+ had no significant impact on the fluorescence lifetime of SQDs. Therefore, it could be considered that there were no dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between them, because Cu2+ could shorten the fluorescence lifetime of SQDs in the two mechanisms [45]. It could be inferred that the fluorescence quenching was probably caused by IFE and then the excitation spectrum, emission spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+ of SQDs were measured (Fig. 5b). A partial overlap between the excitation spectrum of SQDs and the absorption spectrum of Cu2+ could be observed, which well proved the existence of IFE [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the feasibility of applying SQDs to actual samples, SQDs were applied to the detection of Cu2+ in the P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis using the standard addition method in two regions of Yunnan (Xiaguan, Dali and Ninglang, Lijiang). As displayed in Table 4 , the recovery of Cu2+ determined by this method were 92.0%-109%, and the RSD were 1.15%-3.65%, indicating good spiked recoveries and accuracy of the method. These data indicated that the prepared SQDs fluorescent probe could be applied in real samples and could play a role in the actual detection of Cu2+ in the P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis medicinal materials, further confirming the feasibility and reliability of SQDs for the detection of Cu2+ in TCM.

In summary, SQDs nanofluorescent probes were successfully synthesized by the “assemble-fission” method. The prepared SQDs had good dispersion and fluorescence stability. SQDs fluorescence probe based on the effect of IFE was used to detection of Cu2+ in P. polyphylla var. yunnanensis samples, and lower LOD and RSD, wider linear range and better recovery were obtained. This simple and accurate method had great potential for the detection of TCM.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Xiaodong Wen and Yong Liu gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this project from the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 21964001] and [No. 22176021], and the Doctoral Launching Program of Dali University [No. KY2096107940], and the Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects [No. 202101AU070140] and Scientific Research Fund Project of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education [Grant 2022Y818]. The authors especially thank Professor Haifeng Li of Dali University for his help in the identification of traditional Chinese medicine in this work.
